summary: DHH examines whether Apple's commitment to privacy is genuine or merely convenient by focusing on the emerging conflict with Chinese tech giants. While Apple's privacy stance against Facebook costs nothing due to their dominant position, Chinese companies Tencent and ByteDance, backed by the Chinese government, are developing workarounds (CAID) to circumvent iOS 14.5's tracking protections. With China generating over $20 billion per quarter for Apple, the company faces a real test: enforce privacy principles and risk losing the Chinese market, or compromise and undermine its credibility. DHH frames this as Apple's first true privacy test where principles actually carry a price tag.
key_points: Apple's privacy stance against Facebook is essentially free since Apple holds all the leverage in that relationship
Chinese tech giants backed by the state have developed CAID to bypass iOS 14.5 tracking protections, forcing Apple into a genuine dilemma
China represents 20% of Apple's revenue, but Apple is replaceable there by local competitors like Huawei and Xiaomi
Letting Chinese companies skirt privacy rules while enforcing them on Facebook creates an antitrust vulnerability and credibility crisis
Most corporate principles are untested because they never conflict with profit—Apple's are about to be tested for real
money_quotes: It's easy to commit to principles when they don't cost you anything. That's why most mission statements ring so hollow.
The flowery mission statement is usually meant as a fig leaf over the real mission: grow as fast as possible / make as much money as possible.
Standing for something is a risk, so it takes courage and conviction.
Apple has been able to own the principle of privacy for free.

Chinese tech giants, with the explicit backing of the Chinese government, are telling Apple that they're not going to comply with the new privacy controls, and basically daring 'and what are you going to do about it??'
Apple will be forced to weigh the value of its commitment to privacy against the value of billions in Chinese revenue.
Cook already demonstrated with his pandering to the Trump administration that he has no qualms about moral compromises if it means an economic advantage for Apple.
So what's it gonna be, Apple? The purse or the principles?
key_insight: Corporate principles only mean something when they cost you money, and Apple's privacy commitment is about to face its first real test in China where billions of dollars hang in the balance.
